texts.title: Tilbury speech ‚ÄĒ heart and stomach of a king
texts.source: Elizabeth I, Tilbury address, 9 August 1588; transmitted in Camden, Annales (1615) and Collected Works scholarly editions
texts.transliteration: I know I have the body of a weak and feeble woman; but I have the heart and stomach of a king, and of a king of England too.
texts.translation: I know I have the body of a weak and feeble woman; but I have the heart and stomach of a king, and of a king of England too.

texts.pronunciation_model: reconstructed Early Modern English (Tudor court register, c. 1588)
texts.notes: Attested wartime address; manuscript variants exist ‚ÄĒ this line follows standard scholarly edition wording.

texts.title: Tilbury speech ‚ÄĒ loyal hearts
texts.source: Elizabeth I, Tilbury address, 9 August 1588; Camden / Collected Works tradition
texts.transliteration: I thank my loyal people, and from my heart I assure you that no prince ever loved his subjects better.
texts.translation: I thank my loyal people, and from my heart I assure you that no prince ever loved his subjects better.
texts.line_ipa: [a…™ őł√¶Ňčk ma…™ ňąl…Ē…™…ôl ňąpiňźpl, …ônd fr…ím ma…™ h…Ďňźt a…™ …ôňą É ä…ô juňź √į√¶t n…ô ä pr…™ns ňą…õv…ô l Ćvd h…™z ňąs Ćbd í…™kts ňąb…õt…ô]
texts.pronunciation_model: reconstructed Early Modern English (Tudor court register, c. 1588)
texts.notes: Attested Tilbury passage; wording follows near-contemporary historiographic transmission.

texts.title: Golden Speech (1601) ‚ÄĒ crown and glory
texts.source: Elizabeth I, Golden Speech to Parliament, 30 November 1601; parliamentary record / Collected Works
texts.transliteration: To be a king and wear a crown is a thing more glorious to them that see it than it is pleasant to them that bear it.
texts.translation: To be a king and wear a crown is a thing more glorious to them that see it than it is pleasant to them that bear it.
